title: Slab-built oval soap dish
subtitle: A flat slab cut to shape and raised at the edges for drainage
excerpt: An oval soap dish built from a rolled slab with a textured surface and slightly raised rim. Air-dry clay is not waterproof, so this dish is for dry soap display or holding small dry objects; not for use in a wet area without sealing.
sourceNotes: Slab-building technique derived from first principles. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry.

content.text: A slab soap dish is one of the most satisfying first slab projects: one piece of clay rolled flat, cut to an oval, textured if you like, and left to dry. The slightly raised rim keeps the soap from sliding off the edge. Air-dry clay works without a kiln; seal it with PVA if the dish will be kept in a bathroom. Do not place in a wet area without sealing: air-dry clay absorbs water and will eventually soften.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin
items.substitution: Kitchen rolling pin, reserved for clay use

items.name: Two wooden guide rails, 6 mm thick
items.substitution: Two thick rulers of equal height

content.text: Roll 100 g of clay between the guide rails to produce a
about 14 cm by 10 cm, 6 mm thick. Let the slab rest on the board for 5 minutes before cutting.
Press a lace fragment, fabric, stamp, or leaf into the soft slab now, before cutting. Roll the rolling pin lightly over the texture piece to press the pattern in uniformly. Remove the texture piece.
Cut an oval roughly 13 cm by 9 cm using a fettling knife against a cardboard template, or freehand. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger.

To form a raised rim, place the oval over a folded cloth and gently press the edges downward while supporting the centre. This lifts the perimeter edge slightly. Alternatively, roll four thin coils of clay and attach them around the underside perimeter of the oval with slurry, which raises the centre of the dish above the surface and doubles as drainage feet.
Leave flat on the board. Cover with plastic for the first 4 hours. Once
, trim the edge clean with a knife and sand smooth. Full drying takes one day. Seal with PVA or acrylic varnish if the dish will encounter any moisture.

Sealed and kept in a dry area, this dish holds dry soap without issue. It is not suitable for use in a shower or near a sink where it will be regularly splashed unless sealed with waterproof varnish on all surfaces including the base.

items.fix: Place on a plaster bat, or weight the edges with small pieces of tile as the slab stiffens
items.cause: Slab dried faster on one side

items.fix: At leather-hard stage, run a damp finger along the edge and smooth with a rib
items.cause: Knife was dragged rather than pressed
